summary: DHH draws a parallel between Elon Musk's takeover of Twitter and Steve Jobs' return to Apple in 1997. When Jobs came back, Apple was near death — stock at a 12-year low, hemorrhaging money — and he immediately cut 4,100 employees and killed projects. DHH argues that the press hysteria over Musk's staff cuts echoes the same dire predictions made about Apple's cuts, which proved wrong. He contends that decisive action, even with mistakes, beats organizational paralysis.
key_points: Apple cut 4,100 employees and killed numerous projects immediately after Jobs returned in 1997, when the company was near bankruptcy
Press coverage of Apple's cuts was overwhelmingly negative, warning of lost talent and shaky times ahead — predictions that aged poorly
DHH sees an 'uncanny' parallel between Jobs' Apple turnaround and Musk's Twitter takeover: urgency, customer focus, and willingness to make imperfect decisions
The core argument is that decisive leadership that makes some mistakes beats organizational drift that makes no decisions at all
DHH hedges the comparison while still clearly sympathizing with Musk's approach
money_quotes: Some mistakes will be made. That's good. Because at least some decisions will be made too.
The call to urgency, the focus on the customer, and the willingness to start making decisions knowing some of them will be wrong is uncannily similar.
While everyone seems to be collectively losing their shit right now over Elon's staff cuts, it's worth hearing the echo from Jobs' return back then.
It needed a fundamental reboot, and Steve Jobs delivered just that.
It required some savage cuts to the payroll, to the project portfolio, and to the pride of the company.
The alternative was more of the same, and the same clearly wasn't working.
Maybe before you declare the bird house dead, you ought to let your humility reflect history.
key_insight: Dramatic corporate cuts under decisive new leadership look catastrophic in the moment but may be necessary reboots, and history suggests the naysayers are often wrong.
